chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: The Bohr radius is given by $a_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 h^2}{\pi m e^2}$. Determine the dimensions of the right-hand side.
questions.solution:
The dimensions of the physical quantities are:

Permittivity of free space, $[\varepsilon_0] = [M^{-1} L^{-3} T^4 A^2]$

Planck's constant, $[h] = [M L^2 T^{-1}]$

Mass, $[m] = [M]$

Charge, $[e] = [A T]$

Substituting these dimensions into the right-hand side of the given equation:

$[\text{RHS}] = \dfrac{[\varepsilon_0] [h]^2}{[m] [e]^2}$

$[\text{RHS}] = \dfrac{[M^{-1} L^{-3} T^4 A^2] [M L^2 T^{-1}]^2}{[M] [A T]^2}$

$[\text{RHS}] = \dfrac{[M^{-1} L^{-3} T^4 A^2] [M^2 L^4 T^{-2}]}{[M A^2 T^2]}$

$[\text{RHS}] = \dfrac{[M L T^2 A^2]}{[M A^2 T^2]} = [L]$

Alternatively, since $a_0$ represents the Bohr radius, its dimension must be that of length, which is $[L]$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the wavelength of the radiation emitted by hydrogen during the transition from $n = 10$ to $n = 9$.
questions.solution:
The wavelength of the emitted radiation is given by the Rydberg formula:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$

Given $n_1 = 9$, $n_2 = 10$, and Rydberg constant $R_H = 1.097 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^{-1}$:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left( \dfrac{1}{9^2} - \dfrac{1}{10^2} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left( \dfrac{1}{81} - \dfrac{1}{100} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left( \dfrac{19}{8100} \right)$

$\lambda = \dfrac{8100}{19 \times 1.097 \times 10^7} \text{ m}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{8100}{20.843 \times 10^7} \text{ m}$

$\lambda \approx 388.62 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$

$\lambda \approx 38862 \text{ nm}$

The closest value among the given options is $38860 \text{ nm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the smallest wavelength of radiation that can be emitted by hydrogen.
questions.solution:
The smallest wavelength of radiation emitted by a hydrogen atom corresponds to the maximum energy transition.

This maximum energy transition occurs when an electron falls from $n = \infty$ to $n = 1$ (the series limit of the Lyman series).

Using the Rydberg formula:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$

Substituting $n_1 = 1$ and $n_2 = \infty$:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R \left( \dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{\infty^2} \right) = R$

$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{R}$

Substituting the value of the Rydberg constant $R = 1.097 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^{-1}$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{1.097 \times 10^7} \text{ m} \approx 91.1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 91.1 \text{ nm}$

Thus, the smallest wavelength is approximately $91 \text{ nm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the wavelength of the radiation emitted by hydrogen during the transition from $n = 3$ to $n = 2$.
questions.solution:
Using the Rydberg formula for the wavelength of emitted radiation:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_{H} \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$

For the transition from $n = 3$ to $n = 2$, we have $n_1 = 2$ and $n_2 = 3$.

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left( \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{3^2} \right) \text{ m}^{-1}$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{9} \right) \text{ m}^{-1}$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left( \dfrac{5}{36} \right) \text{ m}^{-1}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{36}{5 \times 1.097 \times 10^7} \text{ m}$

$\lambda \approx 6.56 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 656 \text{ nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the wavelength of the radiation emitted by hydrogen during the transition from $n = 5$ to $n = 4$.
questions.solution:
Using Rydberg's formula for the wavelength of emitted radiation:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$

For the transition from $n = 5$ to $n = 4$, we have $n_1 = 4$ and $n_2 = 5$.

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \dfrac{1}{4^2} - \dfrac{1}{5^2} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \dfrac{1}{16} - \dfrac{1}{25} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \dfrac{25 - 16}{400} \right) = \dfrac{9 R_H}{400}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{400}{9 R_H}$

Substituting the value of the Rydberg constant $R_H = 1.097 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^{-1}$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{400}{9 \times 1.097 \times 10^7} \text{ m}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{400}{9.873 \times 10^7} \text{ m} \approx 40.51 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$

$\lambda \approx 4051 \text{ nm}$

The closest value among the given options is $4050 \text{ nm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the smallest wavelength of radiation that can be emitted by $\text{He}^+$.
questions.solution:
The smallest wavelength of radiation corresponds to the maximum energy transition, which occurs when an electron transitions from $n_2 = \infty$ to $n_1 = 1$.

Using the Rydberg formula:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_H Z^2 \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$

For $\text{He}^+$, the atomic number $Z = 2$. Substituting the values:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_H (2)^2 \left( \dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{\infty^2} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 4 R_H$

$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{4 R_H}$

We know that $\dfrac{1}{R_H} \approx 91.2 \text{ nm}$.

$\lambda = \dfrac{91.2}{4} \text{ nm} = 22.8 \text{ nm} \approx 23 \text{ nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the smallest wavelength of radiation that can be emitted by $\text{Li}^{++}$.
questions.solution:
The smallest wavelength of emitted radiation corresponds to the maximum energy transition, which occurs when an electron transitions from $n = \infty$ to $n = 1$.

Using the Rydberg formula for hydrogen-like species:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R Z^2 \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$

For $\text{Li}^{++}$, the atomic number $Z = 3$. Substituting $n_1 = 1$ and $n_2 = \infty$:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R (3)^2 \left( \dfrac{1}{1^2} - 0 \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 9R$

$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{9R}$

Since $\dfrac{1}{R} \approx 91.2 \text{ nm}$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{91.2 \text{ nm}}{9} \approx 10.13 \text{ nm}$

The closest value among the given options is $10 \text{ nm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Calculate the Rydberg constant by substituting the values of the fundamental constants into its expression.
questions.solution:
The expression for the Rydberg constant is given by:

$R = \dfrac{m e^4}{8 \epsilon_0^2 h^3 c}$

Substituting the values of the fundamental constants:
$m = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$
$e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
$\epsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F m}^{-1}$
$h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$
$c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

$R = \dfrac{(9.1 \times 10^{-31}) \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^4}{8 \times (8.85 \times 10^{-12})^2 \times (6.63 \times 10^{-34})^3 \times (3 \times 10^8)}$

$R = 1.097 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the binding energy of a hydrogen atom when it is in the $n = 2$ state.
questions.solution:
The energy of an electron in the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by

$E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$

For the $n = 2$ state, the energy is

$E_2 = -\dfrac{13.6}{2^2} = -\dfrac{13.6}{4} = -3.4 \text{ eV}$

The binding energy is the energy required to remove the electron from this state to infinity, which is the negative of the total energy.

Binding energy $= -E_2 = 3.4 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the radius and energy of a $\text{He}^+$ ion in the state $n = 1$.
questions.solution:
The radius of the $n^{\text{th}}$ Bohr orbit for a hydrogen-like species is given by

$r_n = 0.529 \times \dfrac{n^2}{Z} \text{ \AA}$

For $\text{He}^+$, the atomic number $Z = 2$. For the state $n = 1$,

$r_1 = 0.529 \times \dfrac{1^2}{2} = 0.2645 \text{ \AA} \approx 0.265 \text{ \AA}$

The energy of the $n^{\text{th}}$ state for a hydrogen-like species is given by

$E_n = -13.6 \times \dfrac{Z^2}{n^2} \text{ eV}$

For $\text{He}^+$ in the state $n = 1$,

$E_1 = -13.6 \times \dfrac{2^2}{1^2} = -13.6 \times 4 = -54.4 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the radius and energy of a $\text{He}^+$ ion in the state $n = 4$.
questions.solution:
The radius of the $n$-th orbit for a hydrogen-like species is given by

$r_n = 0.529 \times \dfrac{n^2}{Z} \text{ \AA}$

For $\text{He}^+$, $Z = 2$ and $n = 4$.

$r_4 = 0.529 \times \dfrac{4^2}{2} = 0.529 \times 8 = 4.232 \text{ \AA} \approx 4.24 \text{ \AA}$

The energy of the $n$-th orbit is given by

$E_n = -13.6 \times \dfrac{Z^2}{n^2} \text{ eV}$

$E_4 = -13.6 \times \dfrac{2^2}{4^2} = -13.6 \times \dfrac{4}{16} = -3.4 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the radius and energy of a $\text{He}^+$ ion in the state $n = 10$.
questions.solution:
The radius of a hydrogen-like ion in the $n^{\text{th}}$ state is given by:

$r_n = 0.529 \times \dfrac{n^2}{Z} \text{ \AA}$

For $\text{He}^+$, the atomic number $Z = 2$. For the state $n = 10$:

$r_{10} = 0.529 \times \dfrac{10^2}{2} = 0.529 \times 50 = 26.45 \text{ \AA} \approx 26.5 \text{ \AA}$

The energy of a hydrogen-like ion in the $n^{\text{th}}$ state is given by:

$E_n = -13.6 \times \dfrac{Z^2}{n^2} \text{ eV}$

For $\text{He}^+$ in the state $n = 10$:

$E_{10} = -13.6 \times \dfrac{2^2}{10^2} = -13.6 \times \dfrac{4}{100} = -0.544 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the first excitation potential for a $\text{He}^+$ ion.
questions.solution:
The energy of an electron in the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen-like ion is given by:

$E_n = -13.6 \dfrac{Z^2}{n^2} \text{ eV}$

For a $\text{He}^+$ ion, the atomic number $Z = 2$.

The energy in the ground state ($n = 1$) is:

$E_1 = -13.6 \times \dfrac{2^2}{1^2} = -54.4 \text{ eV}$

The energy in the first excited state ($n = 2$) is:

$E_2 = -13.6 \times \dfrac{2^2}{2^2} = -13.6 \text{ eV}$

The first excitation energy is the energy required to excite the electron from $n = 1$ to $n = 2$:

$\Delta E = E_2 - E_1 = -13.6 - (-54.4) = 40.8 \text{ eV}$

The first excitation potential is the potential difference required to provide this energy, which is $40.8 \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A hydrogen atom emits ultraviolet radiation having a wavelength of $102.5 \text{ nm}$. Determine the quantum numbers of the states involved in the transition.
questions.solution:
The wavelength of the emitted photon is given by the Rydberg formula:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$

Since the emitted radiation is in the ultraviolet region, it belongs to the Lyman series, which corresponds to transitions to the ground state ($n_1 = 1$).

Substituting $\lambda = 102.5 \text{ nm} = 102.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$ and $R_H = 1.097 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^{-1}$:

$\dfrac{1}{102.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$

$9.756 \times 10^6 = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$

$1 - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} = \dfrac{9.756 \times 10^6}{1.097 \times 10^7} \approx 0.889$

$\dfrac{1}{n_2^2} = 1 - 0.889 = 0.111 \approx \dfrac{1}{9}$

$n_2^2 = 9 \Rightarrow n_2 = 3$

Thus, the quantum numbers of the states involved are $1$ and $3$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the ionization potential for a $\text{Li}^{++}$ ion.
questions.solution:
For a hydrogen-like ion, the ionization energy is given by $E = 13.6 \times Z^2 \text{ eV}$.

For a $\text{Li}^{++}$ ion, the atomic number is $Z = 3$.

Substituting $Z = 3$ in the formula:

$E = 13.6 \times (3)^2 \text{ eV} = 13.6 \times 9 \text{ eV} = 122.4 \text{ eV}$.

The corresponding ionization potential is $122.4 \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A collection of hydrogen atoms is prepared in the $n = 4$ state. Identify the wavelengths of the radiation emitted as the atoms make transitions and return to the $n = 2$ state.
questions.solution:
The possible transitions for a hydrogen atom returning from the $n=4$ state to the $n=2$ state are:
$n=4 \rightarrow n=2$
$n=4 \rightarrow n=3$
$n=3 \rightarrow n=2$

Using the Rydberg formula for the wavelength of emitted radiation:
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R \left( \dfrac{1}{n_f^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_i^2} \right)$
where $R \approx 1.097 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^{-1}$.

For the $n=4 \rightarrow n=2$ transition:
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_1} = R \left( \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{4^2} \right) = R \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{16} \right) = \dfrac{3R}{16}$
$\lambda_1 = \dfrac{16}{3R} \approx 486 \text{ nm}$

For the $n=3 \rightarrow n=2$ transition:
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_2} = R \left( \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{3^2} \right) = R \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{9} \right) = \dfrac{5R}{36}$
$\lambda_2 = \dfrac{36}{5R} \approx 656 \text{ nm}$

For the $n=4 \rightarrow n=3$ transition:
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_3} = R \left( \dfrac{1}{3^2} - \dfrac{1}{4^2} \right) = R \left( \dfrac{1}{9} - \dfrac{1}{16} \right) = \dfrac{7R}{144}$
$\lambda_3 = \dfrac{144}{7R} \approx 1875 \text{ nm}$

Comparing the calculated wavelengths ($486 \text{ nm}$, $656 \text{ nm}$, $1875 \text{ nm}$) with the given options, the closest values are $487 \text{ nm}$, $654 \text{ nm}$, and $1910 \text{ nm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A positive ion with exactly one electron ejects this electron when it absorbs a photon of wavelength $228 \text{ Å}$ or shorter. Determine the ion.
questions.solution:
The ionization of a hydrogen-like ion corresponds to the transition of an electron from $n = 1$ to $n = \infty$.

Using the Rydberg formula:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R Z^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{\infty^2}\right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R Z^2$

Substituting $\lambda = 228 \text{ \AA} = 228 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$ and the Rydberg constant $R \approx 1.097 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^{-1}$:

$Z^2 = \dfrac{1}{\lambda R} = \dfrac{1}{228 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.097 \times 10^7}$

$Z^2 = \dfrac{1000}{250.116} \approx 4$

$Z = 2$

The ion with atomic number $Z = 2$ and exactly one electron is $\text{He}^+$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the maximum Coulomb force that can act on the electron due to the nucleus in a hydrogen atom.
questions.solution:
The Coulomb force between the nucleus and the electron in a hydrogen atom is given by:

$F = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{e^2}{r^2}$

For the force to be maximum, the distance $r$ between the electron and the nucleus must be minimum. This occurs in the first orbit ($n=1$), where the radius is the Bohr radius $r = 0.529 \times 10^{-10}\text{ m}$.

Substituting the standard values:

$F_{max} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^9 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2}{(0.529 \times 10^{-10})^2}$

$F_{max} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2.56 \times 10^{-38}}{0.2798 \times 10^{-20}}$

$F_{max} = 82.3 \times 10^{-9}\text{ N} \approx 8.2 \times 10^{-8}\text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A hydrogen atom in a state with a binding energy of $0.85 \text{ eV}$ undergoes a transition to a state having an excitation energy of $10.2 \text{ eV}$. Determine the quantum numbers $n$ of the upper and the lower energy states participating in this transition.
questions.solution:
The binding energy of a hydrogen atom in state $n$ is given by $B.E. = \dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$.

For the upper state, the binding energy is $0.85 \text{ eV}$.

$\dfrac{13.6}{n_2^2} = 0.85 \Rightarrow n_2^2 = \dfrac{13.6}{0.85} = 16 \Rightarrow n_2 = 4$

The excitation energy of a state $n$ is the energy required to excite the electron from the ground state ($n=1$) to that state.

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n_1^2}\right) \text{ eV}$

For the lower state, the excitation energy is $10.2 \text{ eV}$.

$13.6 \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n_1^2}\right) = 10.2$

$1 - \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} = \dfrac{10.2}{13.6} = 0.75$

$\dfrac{1}{n_1^2} = 0.25 \Rightarrow n_1^2 = 4 \Rightarrow n_1 = 2$

The quantum numbers of the upper and lower energy states are $4$ and $2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A hydrogen atom in a state with a binding energy of $0.85 \text{ eV}$ undergoes a transition to a state having an excitation energy of $10.2 \text{ eV}$. Calculate the wavelength of the emitted radiation.
questions.solution:
The binding energy of an electron in the $n^{\text{th}}$ state of a hydrogen atom is given by $B.E. = \dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$.

For the initial state, $B.E. = 0.85 \text{ eV}$:

$\dfrac{13.6}{n_i^2} = 0.85 \Rightarrow n_i^2 = 16 \Rightarrow n_i = 4$

The excitation energy of a state is the energy required to excite an electron from the ground state ($n=1$) to that state. For the final state, the excitation energy is $10.2 \text{ eV}$:

$E_{n_f} - E_1 = 10.2 \text{ eV}$

$-\dfrac{13.6}{n_f^2} - (-13.6) = 10.2$

$\dfrac{13.6}{n_f^2} = 13.6 - 10.2 = 3.4 \Rightarrow n_f^2 = 4 \Rightarrow n_f = 2$

The energy of the emitted photon during the transition from $n_i = 4$ to $n_f = 2$ is:

$\Delta E = E_4 - E_2 = -0.85 - (-3.4) = 2.55 \text{ eV}$

The wavelength of the emitted radiation is:

$\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{\Delta E} = \dfrac{1242 \text{ eV nm}}{2.55 \text{ eV}} \approx 487 \text{ nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Every time hydrogen emits a photon in the Balmer series, it is followed by another photon in the Lyman series. Find the wavelength corresponding to this latter photon.
questions.solution:
The Balmer series corresponds to the transition of an electron from a higher energy level ($n \ge 3$) to the first excited state ($n = 2$).

After this transition, the electron is in the $n = 2$ state. It will subsequently transition to the ground state ($n = 1$), emitting a photon in the Lyman series.

The energy difference for the transition from $n = 2$ to $n = 1$ is given by:

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{2^2} \right) \text{ eV} = 13.6 \times \dfrac{3}{4} \text{ eV} = 10.2 \text{ eV}$

The wavelength of the emitted photon is:

$\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{\Delta E} = \dfrac{1242 \text{ eV nm}}{10.2 \text{ eV}} \approx 121.7 \text{ nm}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $122 \text{ nm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A hydrogen atom initially in the $n = 6$ state undergoes two successive transitions to reach the ground state. During the first transition, a photon of $1.13 \text{ eV}$ is emitted. Determine the energy of the photon emitted in the second transition.
questions.solution:
The energy of an electron in the $n^{\text{th}}$ orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by $E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$.

For the initial state $n = 6$, the energy is:
$E_6 = -\dfrac{13.6}{6^2} = -0.378 \text{ eV}$

During the first transition, a photon of energy $1.13 \text{ eV}$ is emitted. The energy of the intermediate state $n'$ is:
$E_{n'} = E_6 - 1.13 = -0.378 - 1.13 = -1.508 \text{ eV}$

Comparing this with the standard energy levels of hydrogen, we have $E_3 = -\dfrac{13.6}{3^2} = -1.51 \text{ eV}$. Thus, the intermediate state is $n' = 3$.

The second transition occurs from $n' = 3$ to the ground state $n = 1$. The energy of the ground state is $E_1 = -13.6 \text{ eV}$.

The energy of the photon emitted in the second transition is:
$\Delta E = E_3 - E_1 = -1.51 - (-13.6) = 12.09 \text{ eV} \approx 12.1 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A hydrogen atom initially in the $n = 6$ state undergoes two successive transitions to reach the ground state. During the first transition, a photon of $1.13 \text{ eV}$ is emitted. What is the value of $n$ for the intermediate state?
questions.solution:
Energy of an electron in the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by $E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$.

For the initial state $n = 6$, the energy is $E_6 = -\dfrac{13.6}{6^2} = -0.377 \text{ eV}$.

During the first transition, a photon of energy $1.13 \text{ eV}$ is emitted. The energy of the intermediate state $n'$ is $E_{n'} = E_6 - 1.13 \text{ eV}$.

$E_{n'} = -0.377 - 1.13 = -1.507 \text{ eV}$.

Equating this to the energy formula for the $n'$-th state:

$-\dfrac{13.6}{n'^2} = -1.507$

$n'^2 = \dfrac{13.6}{1.507} \approx 9$

$n' = 3$

The intermediate state is $n = 3$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the energy of a hydrogen atom in the first excited state if the potential energy is taken to be zero in the ground state.
questions.solution:
The total energy of an electron in the $n^{\text{th}}$ orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by $E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$.

The potential energy is twice the total energy, so $U_n = 2E_n = -\dfrac{27.2}{n^2} \text{ eV}$.

In the ground state ($n=1$), the standard potential energy is $U_1 = -27.2 \text{ eV}$.

If the potential energy in the ground state is taken to be zero, the reference level of potential energy is shifted by $+27.2 \text{ eV}$. Since kinetic energy remains unaffected by the choice of reference, the total energy of every state also increases by $+27.2 \text{ eV}$.

The standard total energy in the first excited state ($n=2$) is:
$E_2 = -\dfrac{13.6}{2^2} = -3.4 \text{ eV}$

The new total energy in the first excited state will be:
$E'_2 = E_2 + 27.2 \text{ eV} = -3.4 \text{ eV} + 27.2 \text{ eV} = 23.8 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A hot gas emits radiation exclusively at wavelengths of $46.0 \text{ nm}$, $82.8 \text{ nm}$, and $103.5 \text{ nm}$. Assume the atoms possess only two excited states, and the energy difference between consecutive levels decreases with increasing energy. If the energy of the highest state is taken as zero, determine the energies of the ground state and the first excited state.
questions.solution:
The energies of the emitted photons are calculated using $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$, taking $hc = 1242 \text{ eV nm}$.

$E_a = \dfrac{1242}{46.0} = 27 \text{ eV}$

$E_b = \dfrac{1242}{82.8} = 15 \text{ eV}$

$E_c = \dfrac{1242}{103.5} = 12 \text{ eV}$

The gas has three energy levels: ground state $E_1$, first excited state $E_2$, and second excited state $E_3$. The highest energy transition corresponds to $E_3 - E_1 = 27 \text{ eV}$.

The other two transitions are $E_2 - E_1$ and $E_3 - E_2$, which must be $15 \text{ eV}$ and $12 \text{ eV}$.

It is given that the energy difference between consecutive levels decreases with increasing energy, which implies $E_2 - E_1 > E_3 - E_2$.

Therefore, $E_2 - E_1 = 15 \text{ eV}$ and $E_3 - E_2 = 12 \text{ eV}$.

Taking the energy of the highest state as zero ($E_3 = 0$), we get:

$0 - E_2 = 12 \Rightarrow E_2 = -12 \text{ eV}$

$-12 - E_1 = 15 \Rightarrow E_1 = -27 \text{ eV}$

The energies of the ground state and the first excited state are $-27 \text{ eV}$ and $-12 \text{ eV}$ respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A gas of hydrogen-like ions is prepared in a specific excited state $A$. It subsequently emits photons that have a wavelength equal to the wavelength of the first line of the Lyman series, as well as photons of five other wavelengths. Identify the gas and determine the principal quantum number of the state $A$.
questions.solution:
Let the principal quantum number of the excited state $A$ be $n$.

The total number of different wavelengths emitted is $1 + 5 = 6$.

The number of spectral lines emitted when a gas de-excites from state $n$ to the ground state is given by $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.

Equating this to $6$:

$\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} = 6$

$n^2 - n - 12 = 0$

$(n-4)(n+3) = 0$

Since $n$ must be a positive integer, $n = 4$.

The wavelength of the first line of the Lyman series for hydrogen ($Z=1$) corresponds to the transition from $n=2$ to $n=1$. Its wavelength $\lambda$ is given by:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R (1)^2 \left( \dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{2^2} \right) = \dfrac{3R}{4}$

For a hydrogen-like ion of atomic number $Z$, the wavelength of an emitted photon during a transition from $n_2$ to $n_1$ (where $n_2 \le 4$) is:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R Z^2 \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$

Given that this wavelength is equal to the first line of the Lyman series of hydrogen:

$R Z^2 \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right) = \dfrac{3R}{4}$

$Z^2 \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right) = \dfrac{3}{4}$

Checking for $Z = 2$ ($\text{He}^+$):

$4 \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right) = \dfrac{3}{4}$

$\dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} = \dfrac{3}{16}$

This equation is satisfied for $n_1 = 2$ and $n_2 = 4$, which are valid states since the gas is excited to $n = 4$.

Thus, the gas is $\text{He}^+$ and the principal quantum number of state $A$ is $4$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the maximum angular speed of the electron in a stationary orbit of a hydrogen atom.
questions.solution:
The angular speed of an electron in the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by $\omega_n = \dfrac{v_n}{r_n}$.

The velocity of the electron in the $n$-th orbit is $v_n = \dfrac{v_1}{n}$, where $v_1 \approx 2.18 \times 10^6 \text{ m/s}$.

The radius of the $n$-th orbit is $r_n = r_1 n^2$, where $r_1 \approx 0.529 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$.

Substituting these into the expression for angular speed gives $\omega_n = \dfrac{v_1}{r_1 n^3}$.

For the angular speed to be maximum, the principal quantum number $n$ must be minimum, which is $n = 1$ for the ground state.

Substituting $n = 1$, the maximum angular speed is $\omega_{max} = \dfrac{v_1}{r_1}$.

$\omega_{max} = \dfrac{2.18 \times 10^6}{0.529 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 4.12 \times 10^{16} \text{ rad/s}$.

Rounding to the given options, we get $4.1 \times 10^{16} \text{ rad/s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A spectroscopic instrument is capable of resolving two closely spaced wavelengths, $\lambda$ and $\lambda + \Delta\lambda$, provided that the ratio $\lambda/\Delta\lambda$ is smaller than $8000$. This instrument is employed to examine the spectral lines of the Balmer series of hydrogen. Approximately how many lines can the instrument resolve?
questions.solution:
The wavelength of the lines in the Balmer series is given by the Rydberg formula:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{n^2} \right)$

where $n = 3, 4, 5, \dots$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $n$, we get the wavelength difference between adjacent lines for $\Delta n = 1$:

$\dfrac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda^2} = R_H \dfrac{2}{n^3}$

$\Delta \lambda = \dfrac{2 R_H \lambda^2}{n^3}$

The resolving power of the instrument is given by:

$\dfrac{\lambda}{\Delta \lambda} = \dfrac{n^3}{2 R_H \lambda}$

From the Rydberg formula, for large $n$, we can approximate:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} \approx \dfrac{R_H}{4} \Rightarrow R_H \lambda \approx 4$

Substituting this into the resolving power expression:

$\dfrac{\lambda}{\Delta \lambda} \approx \dfrac{n^3}{2 \times 4} = \dfrac{n^3}{8}$

Given that the instrument can resolve lines if $\dfrac{\lambda}{\Delta \lambda} \le 8000$, we have:

$\dfrac{n^3}{8} \le 8000$

$n^3 \le 64000$

$n \le 40$

The lines in the Balmer series correspond to transitions from $n = 3, 4, 5, \dots, 40$.

The total number of lines that can be resolved is:

$40 - 3 + 1 = 38$

Answer: 38

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Assume that under specific conditions, hydrogen atoms are permitted to undergo only those transitions where the principal quantum number $n$ changes by $2$. Determine the smallest wavelength emitted by hydrogen.
questions.solution:
The wavelength of the emitted photon is given by the Rydberg formula:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$

For the smallest wavelength, the energy difference between the transition levels must be maximum. Given the condition that the principal quantum number changes by $2$, we have $n_2 - n_1 = 2$. The maximum energy difference occurs for the lowest possible values of $n_1$ and $n_2$.

Thus, the transition is from $n_2 = 3$ to $n_1 = 1$.

Substituting these values into the formula:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R \left( \dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{3^2} \right) = R \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{9} \right) = \dfrac{8R}{9}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{9}{8R}$

Using the standard value $\dfrac{1}{R} \approx 91.2 \text{ nm}$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{9}{8} \times 91.2 \text{ nm} \approx 102.6 \text{ nm}$

The closest value among the given options is $103 \text{ nm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Assume that under specific conditions, hydrogen atoms are permitted to undergo only those transitions where the principal quantum number $n$ changes by $2$. Determine the wavelength(s) emitted by hydrogen that fall within the visible range ($380 \text{ nm}$ to $780 \text{ nm}$).
questions.solution:
The visible range of the hydrogen emission spectrum corresponds to the Balmer series, where the final state is $n_f = 2$.

Given the condition that the principal quantum number changes by $2$ ($\Delta n = 2$), the initial state must be $n_i = 2 + 2 = 4$.

The energy difference for the transition from $n_i = 4$ to $n_f = 2$ is given by:

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{n_f^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_i^2} \right) \text{ eV}$

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{4^2} \right) \text{ eV} = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{16} \right) \text{ eV}$

$\Delta E = 13.6 \times \dfrac{3}{16} \text{ eV} = 2.55 \text{ eV}$

The corresponding wavelength $\lambda$ can be calculated using the relation $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{\Delta E}$:

$\lambda \approx \dfrac{1242 \text{ eV nm}}{2.55 \text{ eV}} \approx 487 \text{ nm}$

This wavelength falls within the visible range ($380 \text{ nm}$ to $780 \text{ nm}$). Other transitions with $\Delta n = 2$ (such as $3 \rightarrow 1$ or $5 \rightarrow 3$) fall in the ultraviolet or infrared regions, respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Based on Maxwell's theory of electrodynamics, an electron travelling in a circle ought to emit radiation with a frequency equal to its frequency of revolution. If this rule is applied, what would be the wavelength of the radiation emitted by a hydrogen atom in its ground state?
questions.solution:
The frequency of revolution of an electron in its orbit is given by $f = \dfrac{v}{2\pi r}$.

According to Maxwell's theory of electrodynamics, the frequency of the emitted radiation is equal to the frequency of revolution of the electron.

The wavelength of the emitted radiation is $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{2\pi r c}{v}$.

For a hydrogen atom in its ground state ($n=1$):
Radius of the first orbit, $r = 0.529 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$
Velocity of the electron, $v = 2.18 \times 10^6 \text{ m/s}$
Speed of light, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$

Substituting these values into the expression for wavelength:
$\lambda = \dfrac{2 \times 3.14 \times 0.529 \times 10^{-10} \times 3 \times 10^8}{2.18 \times 10^6}$

$\lambda \approx 45.7 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}$

$\lambda = 45.7 \text{ nm}$

Answer: $45.7 \text{ nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: At a temperature $T$, the average kinetic energy of gas molecules is given by $1.5 kT$. Determine the temperature at which the average kinetic energy of hydrogen molecules equals the binding energy of its atoms. Additionally, state whether hydrogen will remain in molecular form at this temperature. Take $k = 8.62 \times 10^{-5} \text{ eV K}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The binding energy of a hydrogen atom is $13.6 \text{ eV}$.

The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule is given as $1.5 kT$.

Equating the average kinetic energy to the binding energy of the hydrogen atom:

$1.5 kT = 13.6 \text{ eV}$

Substituting the value of the Boltzmann constant $k = 8.62 \times 10^{-5} \text{ eV K}^{-1}$:

$1.5 \times 8.62 \times 10^{-5} \times T = 13.6$

$1.293 \times 10^{-4} \times T = 13.6$

$T = \dfrac{13.6}{1.293 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1.05 \times 10^5 \text{ K}$

At this extremely high temperature, the average kinetic energy of the molecules ($13.6 \text{ eV}$) is much greater than the bond dissociation energy of a hydrogen molecule (which is approximately $4.5 \text{ eV}$).

As a result, the violent collisions between molecules will cause them to dissociate into individual atoms. Therefore, hydrogen will not remain in molecular form.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the temperature at which the average thermal kinetic energy matches the energy required to excite a hydrogen atom from its ground state to the $n = 3$ state. Hydrogen can subsequently emit red light with a wavelength of $653.1 \text{ nm}$. Due to the Maxwellian distribution of speeds, a hydrogen sample emits red light at temperatures significantly lower than the value calculated here. Assume that hydrogen molecules dissociate into atoms.
questions.solution:
The energy required to excite a hydrogen atom from the ground state ($n=1$) to the $n=3$ state is given by:

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{3^2} \right) \text{ eV}$

$\Delta E = 13.6 \times \dfrac{8}{9} \text{ eV} \approx 12.09 \text{ eV}$

Converting this energy into Joules:

$\Delta E = 12.09 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 19.34 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

Since the hydrogen molecules dissociate into atoms, the gas is monatomic. The average thermal kinetic energy of a monatomic gas is:

$K = \dfrac{3}{2} k_B T$

Equating the average thermal kinetic energy to the excitation energy:

$\dfrac{3}{2} k_B T = \Delta E$

$T = \dfrac{2 \Delta E}{3 k_B}$

Substituting the standard value of the Boltzmann constant ($k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$):

$T = \dfrac{2 \times 19.34 \times 10^{-19}}{3 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23}}$

$T \approx 9.34 \times 10^4 \text{ K}$

The closest value among the given options is $9.4 \times 10^4 \text{ K}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: For a hydrogen atom excited to the $n = 2$ state, the average lifetime is $10^{-8} 	ext{ s}$. Determine the average number of revolutions completed by the electron before it transitions to the ground state.
questions.solution:
The time period of an electron in the $n^{\text{th}}$ orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by $T_n = \dfrac{2\pi r_n}{v_n}$.

The radius of the $n^{\text{th}}$ orbit is $r_n = 0.529 \times 10^{-10} \times \dfrac{n^2}{Z} \text{ m}$.

The velocity of the electron in the $n^{\text{th}}$ orbit is $v_n = 2.18 \times 10^6 \times \dfrac{Z}{n} \text{ m/s}$.

For a hydrogen atom, $Z = 1$. In the excited state $n = 2$, the radius and velocity are:

$r_2 = 0.529 \times 10^{-10} \times 4 = 2.116 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$

$v_2 = \dfrac{2.18 \times 10^6}{2} = 1.09 \times 10^6 \text{ m/s}$

Substituting these values to find the time period $T_2$:

$T_2 = \dfrac{2 \times 3.14 \times 2.116 \times 10^{-10}}{1.09 \times 10^6} \approx 1.22 \times 10^{-15} \text{ s}$

The average lifetime of the electron in the $n = 2$ state is $t = 10^{-8} \text{ s}$.

The average number of revolutions $N$ completed before transitioning to the ground state is the total time divided by the time period:

$N = \dfrac{t}{T_2} = \dfrac{10^{-8}}{1.22 \times 10^{-15}} \approx 8.2 \times 10^6$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: For hydrogen-like atoms and ions, the ratio of the magnetic dipole moment to the angular momentum ($l = m v r$) is a universal constant. Determine the value of this constant.
questions.solution:
The magnetic dipole moment of an electron revolving in a circular orbit is given by $M = I A$.

The equivalent current is $I = \dfrac{e}{T} = \dfrac{e v}{2 \pi r}$ and the area of the orbit is $A = \pi r^2$.

$M = \left( \dfrac{e v}{2 \pi r} \right) (\pi r^2) = \dfrac{e v r}{2}$

The angular momentum of the electron is $L = m_e v r$.

The ratio of the magnetic dipole moment to the angular momentum is:

$\dfrac{M}{L} = \dfrac{\dfrac{e v r}{2}}{m_e v r} = \dfrac{e}{2 m_e}$

Substituting the values $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ and $m_e = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$:

$\dfrac{M}{L} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31}} = \dfrac{1.6}{18.2} \times 10^{12} \approx 8.8 \times 10^{10} \text{ C kg}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A light beam with wavelengths distributed uniformly between $450 \text{ nm}$ and $550 \text{ nm}$ is passed through a sample of hydrogen gas. Which wavelength will exhibit the least intensity in the transmitted beam?
questions.solution:
The least intensity in the transmitted beam corresponds to the wavelength that is absorbed by the hydrogen gas.

The given wavelength range is $450 \text{ nm}$ to $550 \text{ nm}$, which falls in the visible region. Absorption in the visible region corresponds to the Balmer series, where the electron transitions from the $n = 2$ state to a higher energy level.

The energy of an electron in the $n^{\text{th}}$ orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by:

$E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$

For the $n = 2$ state, $E_2 = -3.4 \text{ eV}$.

For the $n = 3$ state, $E_3 = -1.51 \text{ eV}$. The energy difference is $\Delta E = 3.4 - 1.51 = 1.89 \text{ eV}$, which corresponds to $\lambda = \dfrac{1242}{1.89} \approx 657 \text{ nm}$ (outside the given range).

For the $n = 4$ state, $E_4 = -0.85 \text{ eV}$. The energy difference for the transition from $n = 2$ to $n = 4$ is:

$\Delta E = E_4 - E_2 = -0.85 - (-3.4) = 2.55 \text{ eV}$

The corresponding wavelength is given by:

$\lambda = \dfrac{1242 \text{ eV nm}}{\Delta E} = \dfrac{1242}{2.55} \approx 487 \text{ nm}$

Since $487 \text{ nm}$ lies within the given range of $450 \text{ nm}$ to $550 \text{ nm}$, this wavelength will be absorbed by the hydrogen gas, resulting in the least intensity in the transmitted beam.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Radiation emitted from the $n = 2$ to $n = 1$ transition of hydrogen atoms is incident on helium ions that are in the $n = 1$ and $n = 2$ states. Determine the possible transitions of the helium ions as they absorb energy from this radiation.
questions.solution:
The energy of the photon emitted during the transition of a hydrogen atom from $n = 2$ to $n = 1$ is given by:

$E = 13.6 \times 1^2 \left( \dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{2^2} \right) = 13.6 \times \dfrac{3}{4} \text{ eV}$

This energy is absorbed by a helium ion ($He^+$) with atomic number $Z = 2$. Let the transition be from state $n_1$ to $n_2$. The energy required for this transition is:

$E' = 13.6 \times 2^2 \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right) = 13.6 \times 4 \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$

Equating the two energies, $E = E'$:

$13.6 \times \dfrac{3}{4} = 13.6 \times 4 \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$

$\dfrac{3}{16} = \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2}$

The helium ions are initially in $n_1 = 1$ or $n_1 = 2$ states.

If $n_1 = 1$:

$\dfrac{3}{16} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} = \dfrac{13}{16}$

This does not yield an integer value for $n_2$, so this transition is not possible.

If $n_1 = 2$:

$\dfrac{3}{16} = \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} = \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2}$

$\dfrac{1}{n_2^2} = \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{3}{16} = \dfrac{1}{16}$

$n_2^2 = 16 \Rightarrow n_2 = 4$

Thus, the possible transition for the helium ion is from $n = 2$ to $n = 4$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Determine the magnetic dipole moment associated with the motion of the electron in a hydrogen atom's ground state.
questions.solution:
The magnetic dipole moment of an electron in the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by $M = \dfrac{e v r}{2}$.

From Bohr's quantization condition, the angular momentum is $L = m_e v r = \dfrac{n h}{2 \pi}$.

Thus, $v r = \dfrac{n h}{2 \pi m_e}$.

Substituting this into the expression for magnetic moment:

$M = \dfrac{e}{2} \left( \dfrac{n h}{2 \pi m_e} \right) = \dfrac{n e h}{4 \pi m_e}$

For the ground state, $n = 1$.

$M = \dfrac{e h}{4 \pi m_e}$

Substituting the standard values $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$, $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$, and $m_e = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$:

$M = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31}}$

$M \approx 9.27 \times 10^{-24} \text{ A m}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A hydrogen atom in the ground state absorbs an ultraviolet radiation photon of wavelength $50 \text{ nm}$. Assuming the entire photon energy is taken up by the electron, determine the kinetic energy of the ejected electron.
questions.solution:
The energy of the incident photon is given by $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$

Using $hc \approx 1242 \text{ eV} \cdot \text{nm}$ and $\lambda = 50 \text{ nm}$:

$E = \dfrac{1242}{50} = 24.84 \text{ eV}$

The binding energy of an electron in the ground state of a hydrogen atom is $13.6 \text{ eV}$.

Using the photoelectric equation, the kinetic energy of the ejected electron is:

$K = E - W$

$K = 24.84 \text{ eV} - 13.6 \text{ eV} = 11.24 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A parallel beam of light of wavelength $100 \text{ nm}$ passes through a sample of atomic hydrogen gas in the ground state. Assume that when a photon supplies a portion of its energy to a hydrogen atom, the remaining energy emerges as another photon travelling in the same direction as the incident photon. Disregarding the light emitted by the excited hydrogen atoms in the direction of the incident beam, what wavelengths can be observed in the transmitted beam?
questions.solution:
Energy of the incident photon is given by:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV nm}}{100 \text{ nm}} = 12.4 \text{ eV}$

The energy levels of a hydrogen atom are $E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$.

The energy required to excite a hydrogen atom from the ground state ($n=1$) to higher states is:
For $n=2$: $\Delta E_{12} = 13.6 \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{4}\right) = 10.2 \text{ eV}$
For $n=3$: $\Delta E_{13} = 13.6 \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{9}\right) \approx 12.08 \text{ eV}$
For $n=4$: $\Delta E_{14} = 13.6 \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{16}\right) = 12.75 \text{ eV}$

Since the incident photon energy ($12.4 \text{ eV}$) is less than $12.75 \text{ eV}$, it can only excite the atom to $n=2$ or $n=3$.

When the atom is excited to $n=2$, the remaining energy of the photon is:
$E_2' = 12.4 - 10.2 = 2.2 \text{ eV}$
The wavelength of this transmitted photon is:
$\lambda_2' = \dfrac{1240}{2.2} \approx 563 \text{ nm} \approx 560 \text{ nm}$

When the atom is excited to $n=3$, the remaining energy of the photon is:
$E_3' = 12.4 - 12.08 = 0.32 \text{ eV}$
The wavelength of this transmitted photon is:
$\lambda_3' = \dfrac{1240}{0.32} = 3875 \text{ nm} \approx 3880 \text{ nm}$

The unabsorbed incident photons will also pass through the gas without interacting, so the original $100 \text{ nm}$ wavelength will still be present in the transmitted beam.

Therefore, the wavelengths observed in the transmitted beam are $100 \text{ nm}$, $560 \text{ nm}$, and $3880 \text{ nm}$.

Answer: $100 \text{ nm}$, $560 \text{ nm}$, $3880 \text{ nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A parallel beam of light of wavelength $100 \text{ nm}$ passes through a sample of atomic hydrogen gas in the ground state. Assume that when a photon supplies a portion of its energy to a hydrogen atom, the remaining energy emerges as another photon travelling in the same direction as the incident photon. A radiation detector is positioned near the gas to detect radiation propagating perpendicular to the incident beam. Find the wavelengths of radiation that the detector might register.
questions.solution:
The energy of the incident photon is given by:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1242 \text{ eV nm}}{100 \text{ nm}} = 12.42 \text{ eV}$

The energy required to excite a hydrogen atom from the ground state ($n=1$) to higher energy states is:

$\Delta E_{1 \rightarrow 2} = 13.6 \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2^2}\right) = 10.2 \text{ eV}$

$\Delta E_{1 \rightarrow 3} = 13.6 \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{3^2}\right) = 12.09 \text{ eV}$

$\Delta E_{1 \rightarrow 4} = 13.6 \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{4^2}\right) = 12.75 \text{ eV}$

Since the incident photon energy ($12.42 \text{ eV}$) is greater than $12.09 \text{ eV}$ but less than $12.75 \text{ eV}$, the hydrogen atoms can be excited to the $n=2$ and $n=3$ states. The remaining energy is carried by photons moving in the forward direction, which are not detected by the perpendicular detector.

The excited atoms will de-excite and emit photons in all directions. The possible emission transitions are $3 \rightarrow 1$, $2 \rightarrow 1$, and $3 \rightarrow 2$.

The wavelengths of the emitted photons are calculated as follows:

For $3 \rightarrow 1$: $\lambda_{31} = \dfrac{1242}{12.09} \approx 103 \text{ nm}$

For $2 \rightarrow 1$: $\lambda_{21} = \dfrac{1242}{10.2} \approx 121 \text{ nm}$

For $3 \rightarrow 2$: $\lambda_{32} = \dfrac{1242}{1.89} \approx 657 \text{ nm}$ (closest to $654 \text{ nm}$ in the given options)

Therefore, the detector will register wavelengths of $103 \text{ nm}$, $121 \text{ nm}$, and $654 \text{ nm}$.

Answer: $103 \text{ nm}$, $121 \text{ nm}$, $654 \text{ nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Photoelectrons are ejected from a cesium surface ($\Phi = 1.9 \text{ eV}$) by a beam of monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda$. These photoelectrons are subsequently directed to collide with hydrogen atoms in the ground state. Determine the maximum value of $\lambda$ such that the hydrogen atoms may be ionized.
questions.solution:
The minimum kinetic energy required by a photoelectron to ionize a hydrogen atom in its ground state is equal to the ionization energy of hydrogen, which is $13.6 \text{ eV}$.

According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of the ejected photoelectrons is given by

$K_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

For the photoelectrons to be able to ionize the hydrogen atoms, their kinetic energy must be at least $13.6 \text{ eV}$. Therefore,

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi \ge 13.6 \text{ eV}$

Substituting the given work function of cesium $\Phi = 1.9 \text{ eV}$,

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - 1.9 \text{ eV} \ge 13.6 \text{ eV}$

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda} \ge 15.5 \text{ eV}$

The maximum wavelength corresponds to the minimum energy required:

$\lambda_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{hc}{15.5 \text{ eV}}$

Using $hc = 1240 \text{ eV nm}$,

$\lambda_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{1240}{15.5} \text{ nm} = 80 \text{ nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A beam of monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda$ is used to eject photoelectrons from a cesium surface ($\Phi = 1.9 \text{ eV}$). The ejected photoelectrons are then made to collide with ground-state hydrogen atoms. Find the maximum value of $\lambda$ for which the hydrogen atoms may get excited from the ground state to the first excited state.
questions.solution:
The energy required to excite a hydrogen atom from the ground state ($n=1$) to the first excited state ($n=2$) is given by:

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{2^2} \right) \text{ eV} = 10.2 \text{ eV}$

For the ejected photoelectrons to excite the hydrogen atoms, their maximum kinetic energy must be at least equal to this excitation energy. To find the maximum wavelength $\lambda$ of the incident light, we consider the case where the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is exactly $10.2 \text{ eV}$.

Using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$K_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

Substituting the given values:

$10.2 \text{ eV} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - 1.9 \text{ eV}$

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = 10.2 \text{ eV} + 1.9 \text{ eV} = 12.1 \text{ eV}$

Using the standard approximation $hc \approx 1240 \text{ eV} \cdot \text{nm}$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{1240}{12.1} \text{ nm} \approx 102.4 \text{ nm}$

The closest value among the given options is $102 \text{ nm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda$ ejects photoelectrons from a cesium surface having a work function of $\Phi = 1.9 \text{ eV}$. These photoelectrons are made to collide with hydrogen atoms initially in the ground state. Calculate the maximum value of $\lambda$ so that the excited hydrogen atoms may subsequently emit visible light.
questions.solution:
For hydrogen atoms to emit visible light, they must be excited to at least the $n=3$ state, as visible light corresponds to the Balmer series (transitions to $n=2$).

The minimum energy required to excite a hydrogen atom from the ground state ($n=1$) to $n=3$ is:

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{3^2} \right) = 13.6 \times \dfrac{8}{9} = 12.09 \text{ eV}$

The photoelectrons must have a maximum kinetic energy $K_{\text{max}}$ of at least $12.09 \text{ eV}$ to cause this excitation.

Using the photoelectric equation:

$K_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

Substituting the known values and $hc \approx 1242 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}$:

$12.09 = \dfrac{1242}{\lambda} - 1.9$

$\dfrac{1242}{\lambda} = 13.99$

$\lambda = \dfrac{1242}{13.99} \approx 88.8 \text{ nm}$

The closest maximum wavelength from the given options is $89 \text{ nm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Emitted from an electron gun with almost zero velocity, electrons are accelerated by an electric field $E$ over a distance of $1.0 \text{ m}$. These electrons are subsequently scattered by a sample of atomic hydrogen in its ground state. Determine the minimum value of $E$ required for the hydrogen to emit red light of wavelength $656.3 \text{ nm}$.
questions.solution:
The wavelength of the emitted red light is $656.3 \text{ nm}$, which corresponds to the Balmer series transition from $n = 3$ to $n = 2$.

For the hydrogen atom to emit this light, it must first be excited from its ground state ($n = 1$) to at least the $n = 3$ state.

The energy required for this excitation is:

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{3^2} \right) \text{ eV}$

$\Delta E = 13.6 \times \dfrac{8}{9} \text{ eV} \approx 12.1 \text{ eV}$

The kinetic energy gained by an electron accelerated by an electric field $E$ over a distance $d$ is given by $K = eEd$.

To excite the hydrogen atom, the kinetic energy of the electron must be at least equal to the excitation energy:

$eEd = 12.1 \text{ eV}$

Given $d = 1.0 \text{ m}$, we have:

$e \times E \times 1.0 = 12.1 \text{ eV}$

$E = 12.1 \text{ V/m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A neutron with a kinetic energy of $12.5 \text{ eV}$ strikes a stationary hydrogen atom. Neglecting the mass difference between the neutron and the hydrogen atom, and assuming the neutron's line of motion remains unchanged, determine the possible kinetic energies of the neutron following the collision.
questions.solution:
Let the mass of the neutron and the hydrogen atom be $m$.

Initial kinetic energy of the neutron, $K = 12.5 \text{ eV}$.

The maximum energy that can be lost in the collision occurs when the collision is perfectly inelastic.

Maximum loss in kinetic energy, $\Delta K_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu v_{rel}^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{m \cdot m}{m + m} \right) v^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 \right) = \dfrac{K}{2}$

$\Delta K_{max} = \dfrac{12.5}{2} = 6.25 \text{ eV}$

The minimum energy required to excite a hydrogen atom from its ground state ($n=1$) to the first excited state ($n=2$) is $13.6 \text{ eV} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{4}\right) = 10.2 \text{ eV}$.

Since the maximum available energy ($6.25 \text{ eV}$) is less than the minimum excitation energy ($10.2 \text{ eV}$), the hydrogen atom cannot be excited. Thus, the collision must be perfectly elastic.

The problem states that the neutron's line of motion remains unchanged, which implies a head-on (one-dimensional) collision. In a perfectly elastic head-on collision between two bodies of equal mass, the bodies exchange their velocities.

Since the hydrogen atom was initially at rest, the neutron will come to rest after the collision.

Therefore, the final kinetic energy of the neutron is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A hydrogen atom travelling at a speed $v$ collides with a stationary hydrogen atom. Determine the minimum value of $v$ such that one of the atoms could become ionized. The mass of a hydrogen atom is $1.67 	imes 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$.
questions.solution:
Let the mass of the hydrogen atom be $m$ and its initial speed be $v$.

For the maximum loss of kinetic energy, the collision must be perfectly inelastic. By conservation of linear momentum, the final common velocity $v'$ of the two atoms is given by:

$mv = (m + m)v' \Rightarrow v' = \dfrac{v}{2}$

The maximum loss in kinetic energy $\Delta K$ is:

$\Delta K = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 - \dfrac{1}{2}(2m)\left(\dfrac{v}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{4}mv^2$

For one of the hydrogen atoms to be ionized, this loss in kinetic energy must be at least equal to the ionization energy of a hydrogen atom, which is $13.6 \text{ eV}$.

$\dfrac{1}{4}mv^2 \ge 13.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

$v \ge \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 13.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{m}}$

Substituting $m = 1.67 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$:

$v \ge \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 21.76 \times 10^{-19}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}}}$

$v \ge \sqrt{52.12 \times 10^8}$

$v \ge 7.2 \times 10^4 \text{ m/s}$

The minimum value of $v$ is $7.2 \times 10^4 \text{ m/s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Travelling with a speed $v$, a neutron strikes a hydrogen atom in the ground state that is moving towards it with the same speed. Determine the minimum speed of the neutron for which an inelastic (completely or partially) collision may occur. The mass of the neutron $\approx$ mass of the hydrogen atom $= 1.67 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$.
questions.solution:
The neutron and the hydrogen atom have the same mass $m$ and move towards each other with the same speed $v$.

Since they have equal and opposite momenta, the center of mass of the system is at rest. The total kinetic energy of the system is available for the inelastic collision.

The total kinetic energy is given by:

$K = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 + \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = mv^2$

For an inelastic collision to occur, this kinetic energy must be at least equal to the minimum excitation energy of the hydrogen atom, which corresponds to the transition from the ground state ($n=1$) to the first excited state ($n=2$).

The excitation energy is:

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{2^2} \right) \text{ eV} = 10.2 \text{ eV}$

Equating the kinetic energy to the excitation energy:

$mv^2 = 10.2 \text{ eV}$

Converting the energy into Joules:

$10.2 \text{ eV} = 10.2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 1.632 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}$

Substituting the mass $m = 1.67 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$:

$v^2 = \dfrac{1.632 \times 10^{-18}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}} \approx 9.772 \times 10^8 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$

$v = \sqrt{9.772 \times 10^8} \approx 3.126 \times 10^4 \text{ m/s}$

Rounding off, we get $v \approx 3.13 \times 10^4 \text{ m/s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: When a photon is emitted by a hydrogen atom, it carries momentum. Calculate the momentum carried by the photon if a hydrogen atom emits light of wavelength $656.3 \text{ nm}$.
questions.solution:
The momentum of a photon is given by the de Broglie relation:

$p = \dfrac{h}{\lambda}$

Given:
Wavelength $\lambda = 656.3 \text{ nm} = 656.3 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$
Planck's constant $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$

Substituting the values:

$p = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{656.3 \times 10^{-9}}$

$p = \dfrac{6.63}{6.563} \times 10^{-27}$

$p \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg m s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: When a photon is emitted by a hydrogen atom, it carries momentum. A hydrogen atom emits light of wavelength $656.3 \text{ nm}$. Determine the speed at which the atom recoils during this transition. Take the mass of the hydrogen atom to be $1.67 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$.
questions.solution:
By the principle of conservation of momentum, the recoil momentum of the hydrogen atom is equal in magnitude to the momentum of the emitted photon.

$p_{\text{atom}} = p_{\text{photon}}$

$m v = \dfrac{h}{\lambda}$

$v = \dfrac{h}{m \lambda}$

Substituting the given values, with $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$:

$v = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27} \times 656.3 \times 10^{-9}}$

$v = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{1096.02 \times 10^{-36}}$

$v = \dfrac{6.63}{10.96} \approx 0.6 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: When a photon is emitted by a hydrogen atom, it carries momentum. A hydrogen atom emits light of wavelength $656.3 \text{ nm}$. Find the kinetic energy of recoil of the atom. Take the mass of the hydrogen atom to be $1.67 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$.
questions.solution:
The momentum of the emitted photon is given by $p = \dfrac{h}{\lambda}$

By conservation of linear momentum, the recoil momentum of the hydrogen atom is equal in magnitude to the momentum of the photon.

The kinetic energy of recoil of the atom is $K = \dfrac{p^2}{2m} = \dfrac{h^2}{2m\lambda^2}$

Substituting the given values:

$K = \dfrac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34})^2}{2 \times 1.67 \times 10^{-27} \times (656.3 \times 10^{-9})^2} \text{ J}$

$K = \dfrac{43.96 \times 10^{-68}}{3.34 \times 10^{-27} \times 4.3 \times 10^{-13}} \text{ J} \approx 3.05 \times 10^{-28} \text{ J}$

Converting the energy into electron-volts (eV):

$K = \dfrac{3.05 \times 10^{-28}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \text{ eV} \approx 1.9 \times 10^{-9} \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: The light emitted during the transition from $n = 3$ to $n = 2$ in hydrogen is known as $H_\alpha$ light. Determine the maximum work function a metal can possess such that this $H_\alpha$ light is capable of emitting photoelectrons from it.
questions.solution:
The energy of an electron in the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by $E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$.

For the $H_\alpha$ transition from $n = 3$ to $n = 2$, the energy of the emitted photon is:

$E = E_3 - E_2$

$E = -\dfrac{13.6}{3^2} - \left(-\dfrac{13.6}{2^2}\right)$

$E = -1.51 + 3.40 = 1.89 \text{ eV}$

For photoelectric emission to take place, the energy of the incident photon must be greater than or equal to the work function of the metal ($\Phi$).

$\Phi \le E$

Therefore, the maximum work function the metal can have is $\Phi_{\text{max}} = 1.89 \text{ eV}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: An atom recoils when it emits a photon. The energy of the photon and the recoil kinetic energy are derived from the energy difference between the states involved in the transition. Consider a hydrogen atom that changes its state from $n = 3$ to $n = 2$. Determine the fractional change in the wavelength of the emitted light due to this recoil.
questions.solution:
The energy difference between the states for a transition from $n = 3$ to $n = 2$ in a hydrogen atom is given by:

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{3^2} \right) \text{ eV} = 13.6 \times \dfrac{5}{36} \text{ eV} \approx 1.89 \text{ eV}$

Let $E$ be the energy of the emitted photon and $K$ be the recoil kinetic energy of the atom. From the conservation of energy:

$\Delta E = E + K$

From the conservation of momentum, the momentum of the recoiling atom equals the momentum of the photon:

$p = \dfrac{E}{c}$

The recoil kinetic energy is:

$K = \dfrac{p^2}{2m} = \dfrac{E^2}{2mc^2} \approx \dfrac{(\Delta E)^2}{2mc^2}$

The energy of the emitted photon is $E = \Delta E - K$. The wavelength of the emitted photon is $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E}$.

$\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{\Delta E - K} = \dfrac{hc}{\Delta E} \left( 1 - \dfrac{K}{\Delta E} \right)^{-1} \approx \dfrac{hc}{\Delta E} \left( 1 + \dfrac{K}{\Delta E} \right)$

The fractional change in wavelength is:

$\dfrac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda} = \dfrac{K}{\Delta E} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{2mc^2}$

Using the rest mass energy of a hydrogen atom $mc^2 \approx 938 \times 10^6 \text{ eV}$:

$\dfrac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1.89}{2 \times 938 \times 10^6} \approx 1 \times 10^{-9}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Radiation emitted from a hydrogen discharge tube is incident on a cesium plate. Determine the maximum possible kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons. The work function of cesium is $1.9 \text{ eV}$.
questions.solution:
The maximum energy of a photon emitted from a hydrogen discharge tube corresponds to the transition of an electron from $n = \infty$ to $n = 1$.

The energy of this incident photon is:

$E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{\infty^2} \right) = 13.6 \text{ eV}$

According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is:

$K_{\text{max}} = E - \Phi$

Substituting the given work function of cesium $\Phi = 1.9 \text{ eV}$:

$K_{\text{max}} = 13.6 - 1.9 = 11.7 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Due to gravitational attraction, the earth revolves round the sun. Assume that the sun and the earth are point particles with their existing masses, and that Bohr's quantization rule for angular momentum holds true for gravitation. Determine the minimum radius the earth can have for its orbit. (Given: mass of the earth $= 6.0 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$, mass of the sun $= 2.0 \times 10^{30} \text{ kg}$, earth–sun distance $= 1.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}$).
questions.solution:
According to Bohr's quantization rule, the angular momentum of the earth is quantized as:

$M_e v r = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$

The gravitational force provides the necessary centripetal force for the earth's orbit:

$\dfrac{G M_s M_e}{r^2} = \dfrac{M_e v^2}{r}$

$\Rightarrow v = \sqrt{\dfrac{G M_s}{r}}$

Substituting the expression for $v$ into the quantization rule:

$M_e \sqrt{\dfrac{G M_s}{r}} r = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$

$M_e \sqrt{G M_s r} = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$

Squaring both sides, we get the radius of the $n$-th orbit:

$r = \dfrac{n^2 h^2}{4\pi^2 G M_s M_e^2}$

For the minimum radius, $n = 1$. Substituting the standard values ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$, $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^2/\text{kg}^2$) and the given masses ($M_s = 2.0 \times 10^{30} \text{ kg}$, $M_e = 6.0 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$):

$r = \dfrac{(1)^2 \times (6.63 \times 10^{-34})^2}{4 \pi^2 \times (6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (2.0 \times 10^{30}) \times (6.0 \times 10^{24})^2}$

$r = \dfrac{43.96 \times 10^{-68}}{4 \times 9.87 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 2.0 \times 10^{30} \times 36.0 \times 10^{48}}$

$r = \dfrac{43.96 \times 10^{-68}}{18958 \times 10^{67}}$

$r \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-138} \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Light from the Balmer series of hydrogen is capable of ejecting photoelectrons from a metal. Determine the maximum possible work function of the metal.
questions.solution:
The Balmer series of hydrogen corresponds to electron transitions from higher energy levels $n \ge 3$ to $n = 2$.

The energy of the emitted photon is given by $E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{n^2} \right) \text{ eV}$.

The maximum energy of a photon in the Balmer series is obtained for the transition from $n = \infty$ to $n = 2$.

$E_{\text{max}} = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - 0 \right) = 3.4 \text{ eV}$.

For the photoelectric effect to occur, the energy of the incident photon must be greater than or equal to the work function $\Phi$ of the metal, which implies $\Phi \le E_{\text{max}}$.

Therefore, the maximum possible work function of the metal is $3.4 \text{ eV}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Due to gravitational attraction, the earth revolves round the sun. Assume that the sun and the earth are point particles with their existing masses, and that Bohr's quantization rule for angular momentum holds true for gravitation. Determine the value of the principal quantum number $n$ for the present radius. (Given: mass of the earth $= 6.0 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$, mass of the sun $= 2.0 \times 10^{30} \text{ kg}$, earth–sun distance $= 1.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}$).
questions.solution:
According to Bohr's quantization rule, the angular momentum of the earth is given by:

$L = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$

The angular momentum of the earth revolving in a circular orbit is:

$L = mvr$

The orbital speed $v$ of the earth is obtained by equating the gravitational force to the centripetal force:

$\dfrac{GMm}{r^2} = \dfrac{mv^2}{r} \Rightarrow v = \sqrt{\dfrac{GM}{r}}$

Substituting $v$ into the expression for angular momentum:

$L = m \sqrt{\dfrac{GM}{r}} r = m\sqrt{GMr}$

Equating the two expressions for angular momentum:

$\dfrac{nh}{2\pi} = m\sqrt{GMr}$

$n = \dfrac{2\pi m}{h} \sqrt{GMr}$

Substituting the given values ($G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^2 \text{ kg}^{-2}$, $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$):

$n = \dfrac{2 \times 3.14 \times 6.0 \times 10^{24}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34}} \sqrt{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 2.0 \times 10^{30} \times 1.5 \times 10^{11}}$

$n = \dfrac{37.68 \times 10^{24}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34}} \sqrt{20.01 \times 10^{30}}$

$n \approx 5.68 \times 10^{58} \times 4.47 \times 10^{15}$

$n \approx 25.4 \times 10^{73} = 2.54 \times 10^{74}$

The value of the principal quantum number $n$ is approximately $2.5 \times 10^{74}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Imagine a neutron and an electron bound to each other by gravitational force. Assuming Bohr's quantization rule for angular momentum is applicable in this scenario, determine the expression for the energy of the neutron-electron system.
questions.solution:
The gravitational force provides the necessary centripetal force for the electron to revolve around the neutron.

$\dfrac{G m_n m_e}{r^2} = \dfrac{m_e v^2}{r}$

$v^2 = \dfrac{G m_n}{r}$

According to Bohr's quantization rule for angular momentum:

$m_e v r = \dfrac{n h}{2 \pi}$

$r = \dfrac{n h}{2 \pi m_e v}$

Substituting $r$ in the expression for $v^2$:

$v^2 = \dfrac{G m_n 2 \pi m_e v}{n h}$

$v = \dfrac{2 \pi G m_n m_e}{n h}$

The total energy of the system is the sum of kinetic and potential energy, which is equal to the negative of the kinetic energy:

$E = -K = -\dfrac{1}{2} m_e v^2$

$E = -\dfrac{1}{2} m_e \left( \dfrac{2 \pi G m_n m_e}{n h} \right)^2$

$E = -\dfrac{2 \pi^2 G^2 m_n^2 m_e^3}{n^2 h^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in a region. An electron is projected perpendicular to the field and moves in a circle. Assuming Bohr's quantization rule for angular momentum, determine the smallest possible radius of the electron.
questions.solution:
When an electron moves in a circular path perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field, the magnetic force provides the necessary centripetal force:

$\dfrac{mv^2}{r} = evB$

$mv = eBr$

According to Bohr's quantization rule for angular momentum:

$mvr = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$

Substituting $mv = eBr$ into the quantization condition:

$(eBr)r = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$

$eBr^2 = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$

$r = \sqrt{\dfrac{nh}{2\pi eB}}$

For the smallest possible radius, the principal quantum number $n$ must be minimum, which is $n = 1$. Substituting $n = 1$:

$r = \sqrt{\dfrac{h}{2\pi eB}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Radiation from a hydrogen-discharge tube passes through a filter that transmits only wavelengths greater than $440 \text{ nm}$. This transmitted radiation is then incident on a metal with a work function of $2.0 \text{ eV}$. Determine the stopping potential required to halt the photoelectrons.
questions.solution:
The energy of photons emitted by a hydrogen atom is given by:

$E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right) \text{ eV}$

The corresponding wavelength is $\lambda = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV nm}}{E}$.

Let us check the transitions for the Balmer series ($n_1 = 2$):

For $n_2 = 3 \to n_1 = 2$:
$E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{9} \right) = 1.89 \text{ eV}$
$\lambda = \dfrac{1240}{1.89} \approx 656 \text{ nm}$

For $n_2 = 4 \to n_1 = 2$:
$E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{16} \right) = 2.55 \text{ eV}$
$\lambda = \dfrac{1240}{2.55} \approx 486 \text{ nm}$

For $n_2 = 5 \to n_1 = 2$:
$E = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{25} \right) = 2.86 \text{ eV}$
$\lambda = \dfrac{1240}{2.86} \approx 434 \text{ nm}$

The filter transmits only wavelengths $\lambda > 440 \text{ nm}$. Thus, the $n=5 \to 2$ transition and all higher energy transitions (including the entire Lyman series, which has $\lambda < 122 \text{ nm}$) are blocked.

The transmitted photons are from the $n=3 \to 2$ and $n=4 \to 2$ transitions (as well as higher series like Paschen, which have energies $< 1.51 \text{ eV}$).

The work function of the metal is $\Phi = 2.0 \text{ eV}$.

Since the energy of the $n=3 \to 2$ photon ($1.89 \text{ eV}$) and all higher series photons is less than the work function, they cannot cause photoelectric emission.

The only transmitted photon capable of causing photoelectric emission is from the $n=4 \to 2$ transition, with $E = 2.55 \text{ eV}$.

Using Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is:

$K_{\text{max}} = E - \Phi$

$K_{\text{max}} = 2.55 \text{ eV} - 2.0 \text{ eV} = 0.55 \text{ eV}$

The stopping potential $V_s$ is given by $eV_s = K_{\text{max}}$, which yields:

$V_s = 0.55 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in a region. An electron is projected perpendicular to the field and moves in a circle. Assuming Bohr's quantization rule for angular momentum, determine the radius of the $n$th orbit.
questions.solution:
When an electron moves in a circular path in a uniform magnetic field, the magnetic force provides the necessary centripetal force.

$e v B = \dfrac{m v^2}{r}$

$m v = e B r$

According to Bohr's quantization rule, the angular momentum of the electron is an integral multiple of $\dfrac{h}{2\pi}$.

$m v r = \dfrac{n h}{2\pi}$

Substituting $m v = e B r$ into the quantization condition:

$(e B r) r = \dfrac{n h}{2\pi}$

$e B r^2 = \dfrac{n h}{2\pi}$

$r = \sqrt{\dfrac{n h}{2\pi e B}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Imagine a hypothetical world where angular momentum is quantized as even integral multiples of $h/2\pi$. Based on Bohr's model, what would be the longest possible wavelength emitted by hydrogen atoms in the visible range in such a world?
questions.solution:
In Bohr's model, the angular momentum is given by $L = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$.

Given that angular momentum is quantized as even integral multiples of $\dfrac{h}{2\pi}$, the allowed principal quantum numbers are $n = 2, 4, 6, 8, \dots$

The energy of an electron in the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen atom is $E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$.

For a transition to emit a photon in the visible range (approximately $400 \text{ nm}$ to $700 \text{ nm}$, corresponding to energies between $1.8 \text{ eV}$ and $3.1 \text{ eV}$), the electron must transition to the $n = 2$ state. The allowed transitions to $n = 2$ in this hypothetical world are from $n = 4, 6, 8, \dots$

The longest wavelength corresponds to the minimum energy difference, which occurs for the transition from $n = 4$ to $n = 2$.

The energy difference is:

$\Delta E = E_4 - E_2 = -13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{4^2} - \dfrac{1}{2^2} \right) = 13.6 \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{16} \right) = 13.6 \times \dfrac{3}{16} = 2.55 \text{ eV}$

The corresponding wavelength is:

$\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{\Delta E} \approx \dfrac{1242 \text{ eV nm}}{2.55 \text{ eV}} \approx 487 \text{ nm}$

Answer: $487 \text{ nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: An excited hydrogen atom in state $n$ travels with a velocity $v$ ($v \ll c$). It emits a photon in the direction of its motion and transitions to a lower state $m$. Applying the principles of momentum and energy conservation, determine the frequency $\nu$ of the emitted radiation. Express the result in terms of the frequency $\nu_0$ that would be emitted if the atom were at rest.
questions.solution:
Let the mass of the hydrogen atom be $M$.

From the conservation of momentum:

$Mv = Mv' + \dfrac{h u}{c}$

where $v'$ is the velocity of the atom after emitting the photon.

$v' = v - \dfrac{h u}{Mc}$

From the conservation of energy:

$E_n + \dfrac{1}{2}Mv^2 = E_m + \dfrac{1}{2}Mv'^2 + h u$

$E_n - E_m = \dfrac{1}{2}Mv'^2 - \dfrac{1}{2}Mv^2 + h u$

The energy difference between the states is given by $E_n - E_m = h u_0$. Substituting this and the expression for $v'$:

$h u_0 = \dfrac{1}{2}M \left(v - \dfrac{h u}{Mc} ight)^2 - \dfrac{1}{2}Mv^2 + h u$

$h u_0 = \dfrac{1}{2}M \left(v^2 - \dfrac{2vh u}{Mc} + \dfrac{h^2 u^2}{M^2c^2} ight) - \dfrac{1}{2}Mv^2 + h u$

$h u_0 = -\dfrac{vh u}{c} + \dfrac{h^2 u^2}{2Mc^2} + h u$

Since the mass $M$ of the atom is large, the recoil energy term $\dfrac{h^2 u^2}{2Mc^2}$ is negligibly small and can be ignored.

$h u_0 \approx h u \left(1 - \dfrac{v}{c} ight)$

$ u = u_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{v}{c} ight)^{-1}$

Using the binomial expansion for $v \ll c$:

$ u \approx u_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{v}{c} ight)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in a region. An electron is projected perpendicular to the field and moves in a circle. Assuming Bohr's quantization rule for angular momentum, determine the minimum possible speed of the electron.
questions.solution:
When an electron moves in a circular path perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field $B$, the magnetic force provides the necessary centripetal force:

$e v B = \dfrac{m v^2}{r}$

$r = \dfrac{m v}{e B}$

According to Bohr's quantization rule for angular momentum:

$m v r = \dfrac{n h}{2 \pi}$

Substituting the value of $r$ in the above equation:

$m v \left( \dfrac{m v}{e B} \right) = \dfrac{n h}{2 \pi}$

$\dfrac{m^2 v^2}{e B} = \dfrac{n h}{2 \pi}$

$v^2 = \dfrac{n h e B}{2 \pi m^2}$

$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{n h e B}{2 \pi m^2}}$

For the minimum possible speed, we take the minimum principal quantum number, $n = 1$:

$v_{\text{min}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{h e B}{2 \pi m^2}}$
